bibliographic_data.title: NGC 2419, M92, and the Age Gradient in the Galactic Halo
bibliographic_data.abstract: The WFPC2 camera on HST has been used to obtain photometry of the low-metallicity ([Fe/H] = −2.14), outer-halo globular cluster NGC 2419. Our color-magnitude diagram in (V, V −I) reaches Vlim ≃27.8, clearly delineating the subgiant and turnoff region and about three magnitudes of the unevolved main sequence. A differential fit of the NGC 2419 CMD to that of the similarly metal-poor 'standard' cluster M92 shows that they have virtually identical principal sequences and thus the same age to within 1 Gyr. Previously published studies of many other low-metallicity globular clusters throughout the Milky Way halo show that they possess this same age to within the ∼1 Gyr precision of measurement. The addition of the remote-halo object NGC 2419 to this list leads us to conclude that the earliest star (or globular cluster) formation began at essentially the same time everywhere in the Galactic halo throughout a region now almost 200 kpc in diameter. Thus for the metal-poorest clusters in the halo there is no detectable age gradient with Galactocentric distance. To estimate the absolute age of NGC 2419 and M92, we fit newly computed isochrones transformed through model-atmosphere calculations to the (MV , V −I) plane, with assumed distance scales that represent the range currently debated in the literature. Unconstrained isochrone fits give MV (RR) ≃0.55 ± 0.06 for both clusters, and a resulting age of 14 to 15 Gyr. Incorporating the full effects of helium diffusion would further reduce this estimate by ∼1 Gyr. The first reports of Hipparcos parallax measurements for the lowest-metallicity subdwarfs suggest that the distance scale could be as bright as MV (RR) = 0.15 for [Fe/H] ≃−2, which would require the cluster ages to be less than 10 Gyr; however, the isochrone fits for a distance scale this extreme leave several serious problems which have no obvious solution in the context of current stellar models.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents age analysis of NGC 2419, a low-metallicity outer-halo globular cluster, using HST photometry and isochrones to conclude that the earliest star formation in the Galactic halo occurred at essentially the same time everywhere.
core_content.key_methodology: HST WFPC2 camera was used for obtaining photometric data of NGC 2419. The color-magnitude diagram (CMD) was compared with M92, a similarly metal-poor cluster, to determine their ages using isochrones and model-atmosphere calculations.
outcomes: NGC 2419 and M92 have virtually identical principal sequences in CMD
The age of NGC 2419 and M92 is estimated to be between 14-15 Gyr
No detectable age gradient with Galactocentric distance for metal-poorest clusters
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper uses HST WFPC2 camera photometry on the low-metallicity, outer-halo globular cluster NGC 2419 to determine its age. The color-magnitude diagram (CMD) in (V, V −I) reaches a limiting magnitude of Vlim ≃27.8, revealing the subgiant and turnoff region as well as three magnitudes of unevolved main sequence. By comparing NGC 2419's CMD with that of M92, another low-metallicity cluster, it was found that they have virtually identical principal sequences, suggesting a similar age within 1 Gyr. This finding supports the conclusion that the earliest star formation in the Galactic halo occurred at essentially the same time everywhere. The absolute age of NGC 2419 and M92 is estimated to be between 14-15 Gyr using newly computed isochrones transformed through model-atmosphere calculations, with assumed distance scales debated in literature. The paper also discusses potential implications from Hipparcos parallax measurements for subdwarfs.
